items.front: Under 2-6-1, what weather information must controllers acquire when coming on duty?
items.back: Pertinent weather information, and stay aware of current and forecasted weather needed for ATC duties.

items.front: Under 2-6-2, what are the two PIREP types requiring emphasis?
Under 2-6-2, list three weather phenomena warranting PIREP dissemination.
items.back: Thunderstorms, light to severe icing, wind shear, moderate+ turbulence (including CAT), volcanic eruptions/ash clouds, sulfur gases in cabin, braking action, strong frontal activity, squall lines.

items.front: Under 2-6-3, when is wind described as calm?
items.back: When wind velocity is less than three knots.

items.front: Under 2-6-3, what visibility threshold triggers mandatory tower visibility observations?
items.back: Less than 4 miles prevailing visibility at the tower level or usual point of observation. Use the lower of tower or surface observation.

items.front: Under 2-6-4, what two reference systems are used to define weather/chaff areas to aircraft?
items.back: 1. Azimuth (12-hour clock) and distance from the aircraft. 2. The general width and area coverage in terms of fixes or distance/direction from fixes.

items.front: Under 2-6-5, when can specific weather values be transmitted by a terminal controller?
items.back: Only when: (1) properly certificated as official weather observer, (2) obtained from the official weather station, or (3) obtained from an official ATIS/AWOS/ASOS broadcast.

items.front: Under 2-6-6, what is the geographic scope of hazardous weather advisories?
items.back: 150 NM from the sector or area of jurisdiction. Advisories are NOT required if aircraft on frequency will not be affected.

items.front: Under 2-6-6, list the types of hazardous weather advisories controllers must use.
items.back: AIRMET (except over CONUS), SIGMET, Convective SIGMET (WST), Urgent Pilot Weather Reports (UUA), Center Weather Advisories (CWA).
